title: Opening of the Pākṣika Pratikramaṇa
summary: This step begins the fortnightly (Pākṣika / Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa for a Vidhipaksha-gaccha śrāvaka. Up to the third Āvaśyaka's Vāndaṇā everything follows the daily Devasi rite; then the disciple takes the ādeśa to confess the extended (bṛhad) atichāra of the fourth Āvaśyaka, and the assembly settles into the prescribed posture to hear it.

segments.recitation: (procedural — up to the third Āvaśyaka follow the Devasi rite; then give one khamāsamaṇa and take the ādeśa below)
segments.plainMeaning: Do everything just as in the daily evening pratikramaṇa up to the guru-veneration of the third duty, then bow once and ask permission to continue.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: In the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa, perform the whole ritual procedure exactly as in the daily Devasi pratikramaṇa up to the Vāndaṇā (guru-veneration) of the third Āvaśyaka. Then offer one khamāsamaṇa (bow of homage) and take the ādeśa (formal permission) as given below.
segments.explanation: A bridging direction: the Pakkhī rite shares the Devasi sequence up to Āvaśyaka 3; the single khamāsamaṇa marks the transition into the extended fourth-Āvaśyaka confession.

ritualTerms.gloss: the fortnightly pratikramaṇa, reviewing fifteen days' faults
one of the six obligatory rites; here the third, guru-veneration
the twelve-gesture veneration of the guru (third Āvaśyaka)
a full prostration of homage and request for pardon
the formal call-and-response permission sought before an act

segments.recitation: Icchākāreṇa saṁdissaha bhagavan! Cothā āvaśyaka bhaṇī, guru-parva-bhaṇī pākhī saviśeṣa bṛhad atichāra ālouṁjī?
segments.plainMeaning: The disciple asks the guru's leave to confess the special extended list of transgressions for the fortnightly rite.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: Of your grace, command me, O Bhagavan! For the fourth Āvaśyaka, on this guru's holy day, may I confess the special, extended atichāra (transgressions) of the Pakkhī?
segments.explanation: Spoken by the disciple (śiṣya) as a request for permission; it introduces the Bṛhad Atiyār, the extended confession that replaces the short atichāra in the fortnightly rite.

ritualTerms.gloss: formal request for the guru's permission
obligatory rite; here the fourth, reflection and atonement
the extended confession of transgressions of the twelve lay vows

segments.plainMeaning: The guru grants permission: "Make your confession."
segments.explanation: The guru's (guru) one-word reply granting the requested permission.

ritualTerms.gloss: the act of confessing one's transgressions before the guru

segments.idiomaticTranslation: So be it; I gladly accept your command.
segments.explanation: The disciple's (śiṣya) fixed assent-formula, accepting the guru's instruction before beginning the confession.

ritualTerms.gloss: "I accept / as you wish" — the standard formula of assent

segments.recitation: (procedural — one person stands, recites one Navkar, and recites the Bṛhad Atiyār; the rest sit with the right knee raised through the first eight atichāras, then stand from the ninth and listen attentively)
segments.plainMeaning: One person stands and reads out the long confession after a Navkar, while everyone else sits with the right knee up for the first eight items, then stands from the ninth and listens carefully.
segments.idiomaticTranslation: One person stands, recites a single Navkar, and then recites the Bṛhad Atiyār. All the others, up to the first eight atichāras, sit keeping the right knee raised; from the ninth atichāra onward they stand and listen to the atichāras with full attention.
segments.explanation: A posture and recitation direction: it assigns one reciter and prescribes the assembly's changing posture — seated with right knee raised for atichāras 1–8, standing from atichāra 9.

ritualTerms.gloss: the Namaskāra Mahāmantra, homage to the five Parameṣṭhins
the extended confession of the twelve lay-vow transgressions
a transgression or partial breach of a vow

notes: The native segments spell the term as 'અતિચાર' (atichāra) in segments 02 and 05, whereas the tradition KB notes the book elsewhere uses 'અતિયાર' (atiyār); both are reflected, kept faithful to each occurrence. 'ગુરુપર્વભણી' is rendered 'guru-parva-bhaṇī' (on the guru's holy/festival day) — the exact sense of 'parva' here is mildly uncertain but consistent with the fortnightly (parva-tithi, chaudas) context. Segments 01 and 05 are pure procedural directions, not chanted; their 'recitation' fields give a procedural cue rather than a chant line, per policy. No steps, counts, or postures beyond the native text were added.
